title: SEGMENT 18 — The next decade: patent cliffs, AI base calling, NovaSeq X and beyond

lines.text: SEGMENT 18 — The next decade: patent cliffs, AI base calling, NovaSeq X and beyond
Let me close with some speculation about where the platform and the field are going in the next ten years.
I'll be careful to flag what's confident and what's speculative.
Patent cliff and competition.

Many of Illumina's core patents on the original Solexa chemistry — reversible terminators, bridge amplification on flow cells, cluster-based imaging — have expired in the last few years or are expiring soon.
That's why the competitors I mentioned in Segment 16 have been able to build broadly similar platforms.
The patent landscape used to be a moat around Illumina's business; now it's an increasingly leaky moat.
Over the next decade, we'll see more competitors, more pricing pressure, and probably continued downward movement in cost per base.
Illumina's response has been to invest heavily in next-generation chemistry like XLEAP-SBS that they hope to patent and use as the basis of a new moat.
Whether that strategy works or whether the field commoditizes is a major open question.
AI base calling.
The base calling step — converting the camera images into base sequences with quality scores — has historically been done with hand-tuned algorithms.

Over the last few years, both Illumina and outside researchers have shifted to machine-learning-based base callers, particularly neural networks trained on huge datasets of real reads with known truth.

These ML base callers have meaningfully improved accuracy and quality calibration, particularly for difficult cases like homopolymers, low-density clusters, and long reads where dephasing degrades signal.
The trend is toward all base calling becoming neural-network-based.
The same trend is even more pronounced in long-read sequencing — Oxford Nanopore's accuracy gains over the last few years have come largely from progressively better neural-network base callers.
Expect the AI layer of sequencing to keep improving for years.
The race toward the $100 genome and below.
NovaSeq X put Illumina at roughly $200 per genome at the very high end of volume.
The next milestone is $100 per genome, which Illumina has explicitly discussed as a target.
Below that is $50, then $10.
There's no obvious chemistry limit yet.
The question is mostly about whether competition forces price cuts faster than incremental engineering or whether the field accepts the current cost as adequate.

My guess — and this is genuinely speculation — is that we'll see the $100 genome become routine within five years and the $50 genome within ten, driven by some combination of NovaSeq X's successor, MGI's pricing, and pressure from new entrants.
Population-scale and routine clinical genomics.
As costs drop and clinical pipelines mature, genome sequencing will increasingly become a routine part of medical care.
National-scale projects — Genomics England, the All of Us program, Singapore's PRECISE, Estonia's Biobank — have shown that sequencing hundreds of thousands or millions of citizens is feasible.
Over the next decade, expect more countries to fold genome sequencing into routine national health screening, particularly for newborns, for prenatal care, and for high-risk populations.
The challenge will shift from "can we sequence everyone?
" to "what do we do with the data?
" — questions of interpretation, privacy, equitable access, and the integration of genomic data into clinical decision-making.
The integration with other omics.
Illumina sequencing isn't just for genomes.
The same instruments produce data for RNA-seq, ChIP-seq, methylation profiling, chromatin accessibility, single-cell multiomics, spatial transcriptomics, and more.
As the cost of basic sequencing drops, the bottleneck shifts to the upstream library preps and the downstream analysis.
Expect a flowering of new specialized library prep methods that turn the Illumina platform into a generic read-out device for whatever molecular question you want to ask.
The instruments are general-purpose; the assays built on top of them are increasingly creative.
Long-read convergence.
Whether the next decade sees long-read platforms displace Illumina for routine work, or remain specialized complements, is genuinely uncertain.
If Oxford Nanopore can drive its accuracy up to consistent Q30 and its cost down toward Illumina's, the case for short reads becomes much weaker.
If long reads remain expensive and somewhat noisy, the current division of labor — short reads for variant calling, long reads for assembly and structural variants — will persist.
My guess is that hybrid pipelines will dominate for high-value applications, while short reads will remain the workhorse for high-volume routine genomics.
But this is the kind of prediction that ages poorly.
The deeper shift: sequencing as infrastructure.
Maybe the most important long-term consequence of the Illumina cost trajectory is that DNA sequencing has stopped being a "project" and started being infrastructure.
You don't think about sequencing a sample any more than you think about printing a document or running a database query.

It's a routine operation that happens in the background, with predictable cost and turnaround, and the interesting work is at the layers above — what to sequence, what to analyze, what conclusions to draw.
That infrastructure status is what the platform has enabled.

And the platform itself — the four-step cycle of incorporate, image, cleave, repeat, parallelized across billions of clusters on a glass flow cell — has become so reliable, so cheap, and so widely available that the entire field of modern genomics has grown up around its assumptions.
Let me close with the headline.
Twenty-five years ago, Shankar Balasubramanian and David Klenerman sat in a pub in Cambridge and sketched out a way to detect single nucleotides being added to a DNA strand.
The idea was a piece of basic-science fluorescence chemistry that nobody outside their lab cared about.

Twenty-five years later, the descendants of that idea sequence over fifty thousand human genomes a day worldwide, drive clinical decisions for millions of patients per year, underpin the entire fields of genomics, transcriptomics, and personalized medicine, and have collapsed the cost of reading life's source code by a factor of fifteen million.
That trajectory — from pub conversation to global infrastructure — is one of the great stories of modern biology.
The chemistry is the seed.
The engineering is the tree.
The science the tree enables — population genomics, precision oncology, rapid pandemic response, the routine diagnosis of children with genetic disease — is the fruit.
And the fruit, for the rest of your careers in biology and medicine, is going to keep getting larger and more abundant and more transformative.
Sequencing isn't done changing the world.
It's just getting started.
That's it.
Thank you for your attention.
Questions?
See you next week.
